ICtoolkit 0.1.0
  • Get started
  • Reference
  • Changelog

Compute mBIC2 for model objects

Source: R/compute_mbic2.R
compute_mbic2.Rd

Computes the modified BIC2 criterion (mBIC2) of Frommlet & Bogdan (2013). mBIC2 extends mBIC by subtracting a combinatorial correction that accounts for the number of models of a given size:

compute_mbic2(fit, P = NULL, kappa = 4, ...)

Arguments

fit

A fitted model object of class lm, glm, glmnet, or ncvreg.

P

Integer. Total number of candidate predictors (excluding the intercept). If NULL (the default), inferred from fit.

kappa

Positive numeric scalar controlling the prior expected number of true signals. Must satisfy P > kappa. Default is 4.

...

Currently unused; reserved for future use.

Value

A named numeric scalar (lm/glm) or vector (glmnet/ncvreg) with the following attributes:

fit_class

Character. The primary class of fit.

k

Integer scalar or vector. Number of estimated parameters (including intercept).

P

Integer. The value of P used.

kappa

Numeric. The value of kappa used.

criterion

"mBIC2".

Details

$$\mathrm{mBIC2} = \mathrm{BIC} + 2\,k_{\text{pred}}\, \log\!\bigl(P/\kappa - 1\bigr) - 2\,\log\binom{P}{k_{\text{pred}}}$$

The additional log C(P, k) term partially offsets the mBIC penalty, making mBIC2 less conservative than mBIC when multiple predictors are selected.

For glmnet and ncvreg, mBIC2 is computed at each point on the regularization path (one value per lambda).

References

Frommlet, F., & Bogdan, M. (2013). Some optimality properties of FDR controlling rules under sparsity. Electronic Journal of Statistics, 7, 1328–1368.

See also

compute_mbic(), compute_ebic(), compute_bic(), ic_step()

Examples

set.seed(1)
X <- matrix(rnorm(100 * 20), 100, 20)
y <- X[, 1:3] %*% c(2, -1, 0.5) + rnorm(100)
fit <- lm(y ~ X)

compute_mbic2(fit, P = 20)                 # default kappa = 4
#> [1] 434.401
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 21
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "mBIC2"
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 20
#> attr(,"kappa")
#> [1] 4
compute_mbic2(fit, P = 20, kappa = 2)      # expect fewer signals
#> [1] 466.8382
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 21
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "mBIC2"
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 20
#> attr(,"kappa")
#> [1] 2

# Compare mBIC and mBIC2
compute_mbic(fit, P = 20)                  # more conservative
#> [1] 434.401
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 21
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "mBIC"
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 20
#> attr(,"kappa")
#> [1] 4
compute_mbic2(fit, P = 20)                 # less conservative
#> [1] 434.401
#> attr(,"fit_class")
#> [1] "lm"
#> attr(,"k")
#> [1] 21
#> attr(,"criterion")
#> [1] "mBIC2"
#> attr(,"P")
#> [1] 20
#> attr(,"kappa")
#> [1] 4
